summary: Stancil argues that the public Facebook leak revealed something rare: not just debate about social media, but about the quality of analysis itself. Because most analysis is proprietary, the field obsesses over tools and structures while the actual work stays invisible, making it hard to define or improve what “good analysis” looks like. He concludes that quality is subjective and multi-dimensional, and the way forward is to expose more real analysis so standards can evolve.
money_quotes: In the middle of the analytical field, in the eye of our swirling hurricane about titles and team structures and tooling, is analysis itself.
The analysis we produce is still only in our peripheral vision, at the center a black hole: unknowable on its own, only detectable by its effects.

To extend Randy Au’s woodworking analogy, the veil around analytical work forces us to talk about the saws we prefer, the types of wood we like, and the paths we can take through our apprentice program without talking about the actual chairs we build.
It feels to me, then, that our actual answer is an analytical classic: It depends.

Unless we see their work—unless we figure out how to talk about what’s currently hidden behind NDAs instead of talking about teams and languages and tools and tools and tools and tools and tools—we’ll never know how high the bar could be set.
key_insight: Good analysis can’t be reduced to a single yardstick, and without public examples of real work, the field can’t reliably raise its standards.
